id: bigtech:linux bitten by second severe vulnerability in as many weeks:2026-05-11T22:28:19.000Z
title: Linux bitten by second severe vulnerability in as many weeks
author: Dan Goodin
summary_long: Linux users have been bitten by yet another vulnerability that gives containers and untrusted users the ability to gain root access, marking the second time in as many weeks that a severe threat has caught defenders off guard. The threat, known as Dirty Frag, allows low-privilege users, including those using virtual machines, to gain root control of servers. Attacks are particularly suitable in shared environments, where a server is used by multiple parties. Hackers can also gain root as long as they have access to a separate exploit that gives a toehold into a machine. Source: arstechnica.com. Date: 2026-05-11. Why it matters: outline likely impact on technology, business, or society. What’s next: note near-term developments or signals to watch.

id: bigtech:after banning foreign routers fcc says existing ones can get:2026-05-11T20:48:33.000Z
title: After banning foreign routers, FCC says existing ones can get updates until 2029
author: Jon Brodkin
summary_long: The Federal Communications Commission is relenting a bit on its restrictive router rules, saying it will allow foreign-made routers to receive software and firmware updates until at least January 1, 2029. The FCC also expanded the waiver to cover more types of software updates. Previously, the FCC said routers currently on the market or already sold to consumers could receive security patches and other updates only until March 1, 2027. On Friday, the agency announced a waiver extension that lets devices receive updates until January 1, 2029, and said the waiver may eventually become permanent. Source: arstechnica.com. Date: 2026-05-11. Why it matters: outline likely impact on technology, business, or society. What’s next: note near-term developments or signals to watch.

id: bigtech:ios macos and ipados 265 updates arrive with encrypted rcs m:2026-05-11T17:20:39.000Z
title: iOS, macOS, and iPadOS 26.5 updates arrive with encrypted RCS messaging and more
author: Andrew Cunningham
summary_long: Apple has released version 26.5 of all of its operating systems today: iOS 26.5, iPadOS 26.5, macOS 26.5, watchOS 26.5, tvOS 26.5, visionOS 26.5, and version 26.5 of the HomePod software (whew). None of these are particularly momentous updates, which is pretty normal this late in their lifecycle, but they add a small batch of new features alongside the pile of patches outlined on Apple’s security vulnerabilities page. This is Apple’s first release to support end-to-end encryption for the RCS messaging standard, for example, which, when enabled, can give green-bubble messages some of the same security and privacy advantages that iMessage users have long enjoyed. Encrypted RCS messaging has a “beta” label in this release, and Apple says it’s limited to a subset of supported cellular carriers. Source: arstechnica.com. Date: 2026-05-11. Why it matters: outline likely impact on technology, business, or society. What’s next: note near-term developments or signals to watch.

title: Quantum dot emitter delivers near-identical telecom photons at 40 million per second
summary_long: Quantum technologies, devices that perform specific functions leveraging quantum mechanical effects, could soon outperform their classical counterparts on some tasks. Quantum emitters, devices that release individual particles of light (i.e., photons), are central components of many of these technologies, including quantum communication systems and quantum computers. id: code:connecting the dots for accurate ai:2026-05-12T07:40:00.000Z
title: Connecting the dots for accurate AI
author: Phoebe Sajor
summary_long: At HumanX, Ryan is joined by Philip Rathle, CTO at Neo4j to discuss what knowledge context means for AI agents, how limitations like stale training data make the model-only approach to agents a bad fit for enterprise environments, and how Graph RAG raises the bar for accuracy and reduces context rot by combining vectors with a knowledge graph so agents are more targeted and connected. Source: stackoverflow.blog. Date: 2026-05-12. Why it matters: outline likely impact on technology, business, or society. What’s next: note near-term developments or signals to watch.

id: space:spacex launches intelligencegathering satellites for the nat:2026-05-11T19:50:46.000Z
title: SpaceX launches intelligence-gathering satellites for the National Reconnaissance Office
author: Will Robinson-Smith
summary_long: Updated May 11, 10:48 p.m. EDT (0248 UTC): SpaceX landed its booster on the drone ship. The National Reconnaissance Office flew its 13th mission supporting an intelligence-gathering constellation it calls the “proliferated architecture” on Monday night. As with the first dozen missions, this batch of satellites (of an undisclosed quantity) will fly to orbit on a SpaceX Falcon 9 rocket. Source: spaceflightnow.com. Date: 2026-05-11. id: ai_data:trajprism a multitask benchmark for languagegrounded urban t:2026-05-11T16:17:13.000Z
title: TrajPrism: A Multi-Task Benchmark for Language-Grounded Urban Trajectory Understanding
author: Lihuan Li
summary_long: Urban mobility is naturally expressed both as trajectories in space and as natural-language descriptions of travel intent, constraints, and preferences. However, prior work rarely evaluates these two modalities together on the same real-world trajectories: trajectory modeling often stays geometry-centric, while language-centric mobility benchmarks frequently target route planning and tool use rather than fine-grained, verifiable alignment between text and the underlying route. We introduce TrajPrism, a multi-task benchmark for language-trajectory alignment that unifies (i) instruction-conditioned trajectory generation, (ii) language-driven semantic trajectory retrieval, and (iii) trajectory captioning, together with an evaluation protocol that measures trajectory fidelity, retrieval quality, and language groundedness. We construct TrajPrism by pairing real urban trajectories with judge-filtered language annotations generated under a four-dimensional travel-intent taxonomy. Source: arxiv.org. Date: 2026-05-11. Why it matters: outline likely impact on technology, business, or society. What’s next: note near-term developments or signals to watch.

id: code:github for beginners getting started with oss contributions:2026-05-11T16:00:00.000Z
title: GitHub for Beginners: Getting started with OSS contributions
author: Kedasha Kerr
summary_long: Welcome back to GitHub for Beginners. So far, we’ve discussed GitHub Issues and Projects, GitHub Actions, security, GitHub Pages, and Markdown. This time we’re going to talk about open source software and how to contribute to that community. By the end of this post, you’ll know what open source is, how to find projects to work on, how to read an open source repository, as well as start making your first contributions. Source: github.blog. Date: 2026-05-11. Why it matters: outline likely impact on technology, business, or society. What’s next: note near-term developments or signals to watch.
